overall_summary: The Vesuvius Challenge Surface Detection Competition focused on segmenting ancient scroll surfaces from 3D volumetric data, where success hinged heavily on optimizing topology-aware metrics rather than pure volumetric overlap. Winning approaches predominantly leveraged nnU-Net architectures and custom 3D U-Net variants, often regressing signed distance fields or using skeleton-based losses to better capture boundary continuity. A defining characteristic across top solutions was the reliance on highly iterative, multi-stage post-processing pipelines—ranging from persistence homology tunnel-filling to diffeomorphic warping and local interpolation—to repair topological defects and maximize final scores.
competition_flows: Raw 3D volumetric data fed into multiple nnU-Net models with varying patch sizes and schedules, producing probability maps fused via weighted averaging, thresholded, and refined through a multi-step morphological post-processing pipeline
Pipeline shifts from volume segmentation to instance segmentation driven by boundary clues, applying progressive gap-filling techniques to optimize topology and surface metrics

Raw 3D volumes loaded from npy files, cropped into 160³ patches, and used to train 3D U-Net variants to regress signed distance fields; predictions averaged across checkpoints and TTA flips, thresholded, and refined via a persistence-homology tunnel-filling pipeline

Raw 3D data segmented using a heavily trained nnUNet model, followed by thresholding and component filtering, then refined through two PCA-based topological hole-filling stages and a Betti-2 metric-hacking step
Raw surface imaging data processed and fed into a custom uuNet architecture trained with a skeleton loss and deep supervision for 1000 epochs, followed by TTAx6 and post-processing

Raw 3D scroll volumes processed through a five-stage pipeline comprising initial multi-model segmentation, ensemble learning, two refinement stages, and a diffeomorphic warping stage, followed by median filtering and morphological post-processing

Raw 3D volumetric data processed through two nnUNet models with different patch sizes, ensembled with weighted logits and TTA, then refined via a multi-threshold local interpolation postprocessing pipeline that corrects topology errors using Euler numbers and SVD-based 2D projection

Raw 3D volumes undergo full-axis flipping and high-probability rotation augmentations, fed into a two-stage nnU-Net v2 cascade pipeline trained on the full dataset, processed through custom TTA with in-plane rotations and multi-model checkpoint fusion, and refined via a topology-aware post-processing pipeline using 3D Hessian ridge detection and distance transform

Raw data processed into 192x192 patches, fed into a single-fold nnUNet baseline with TTA and 0.4 tile size inference, followed by a multi-stage iterative post-processing pipeline to produce the final submission
data_processing: On-the-fly SDF target computation via GPU Euclidean distance transforms
External data handled via a binary mask channel indicating label quality
Removing connected components smaller than 1000 voxels
Test-time augmentation via mirroring during inference
Training at 160³ patch size with mixed precision
Random thresholding on the fly during training (0.1 to 0.7) and fixed 0.3 threshold at inference
Hard mask generation followed by Gaussian blur for diffeomorphic stage input
Post-processing with median filtering, binary closing, cavity filling, and component size filtering
Zeroing 3-voxel label 2 borders on volume faces
Custom TTA: mirror flipping and in-plane rotation (±15°)
Training patch size set to 192; inference tile size set to 0.4
Iterative removal of small 6-connected components (<5000 and <10 voxels)
Line normalization via skeletonization, EDT reconstruction to radius 1.5, and block-wise processing
Gap filling and diagonal repair loops
Gaussian filtering on boolean mask followed by >0.5 thresholding
cv_strategies: 4-fold cross-validation tracking Surface Dice, VOI, and topology score on 10x downsampled size
6 hold-out volumes for local CV Dice with 75/25 train/holdout split validated on 130 volumes
All folds used for training with validation driven by visual tuning and public/private LB submissions
Trained on entire dataset (abandoned K-Fold CV)
ensembling: Weighted ensemble of four nnU-Net models fused via post-softmax probabilities (with logits fusion noted as superior)
Raw SDF logits averaged across all model checkpoints and TTA augmentations per sample
TTAx6 and post-processing applied during inference
Initial predictions from three models combined via a second-stage ResEnc-L UNet trained on a 4-channel input to learn complementarity
Logits from 128³ and 160³ nnUNet models summed with a 60/40 weight favoring the larger patch
Multi-model ensemble fusing equal-weight or weighted probability maps from checkpoints across different iteration steps
Single fold inference combined with Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) with no model ensembling applied
insights: A multi-step post-processing pipeline targeting holes and cavities was essential for maximizing the Dice score.
Optimizing for the public leaderboard can be misleading; larger thresholds and logits fusion proved superior for the private leaderboard.
AI-assisted coding can effectively resolve resource constraints, enabling more complex ensemble pipelines.
Strong boundary clues enable a direct jump to instance segmentation via multi-class pixel labeling, avoiding semantic segmentation pitfalls.
The task is fundamentally scroll object detection where continuous surfaces without holes are critical, making the topology score the most important metric.
Ground truth is weakly supervised, focusing on the surface and one side of the scroll, sometimes extending into the air.
SDF regression outperforms binary classification for optimizing topology and boundary metrics.
Gaussian weighting effectively concentrates supervision near the skeleton while downweighting distant background.
Iterative tunnel filling with bridge detection is the most impactful post-processing step for improving topology scores.
nnUNet demonstrates exceptional generalization capabilities for segmentation tasks.
The topological score is extremely sensitive to minor structural holes.
Manipulating Betti-2 counts can effectively balance topological metrics despite official claims of limited impact.
Separating adhering sheets remains a significant unresolved challenge in this domain.
ChatGPT can effectively generate custom PyTorch architectures and loss functions when provided with host specifications and community comments.
Deep supervision and extended training (1000 epochs) significantly boost performance.
Using multiple GPUs during inference accelerates processing without degrading results.
Pretraining on external scroll data significantly accelerated convergence during fine-tuning.
Intentionally introducing errors via low thresholds and Gaussian blur helps the diffeomorphic model learn to correct topology and thickness.
The diffeomorphic stage's topology-aware correction using SDF shift prediction effectively prevents flow folding issues common in optical flow methods.
Public leaderboard metrics can be heavily influenced by favorable sampling effects, making metric-specific validation crucial before final submission.
Postprocessing can be the primary driver of rank in topology-aware segmentation challenges.
The topology metric relies on persistence homology, meaning component location matters as much as component count.
Local interpolation outperforms global interpolation for avoiding label 2 artifacts.
Iteratively increasing probability thresholds naturally separates merged sheet components without manual parameter tuning.
Asymmetric patch sizing effectively balances training batch efficiency with a larger inference receptive field.
Integrating clDice Loss alongside standard losses significantly improves the preservation of complex tubular and mesh-like topological structures.
Training directly on the entire dataset yields more robust generalization than traditional K-Fold cross-validation for this task.
Custom TTA with ±15° in-plane rotation aligns optimally with the ±30° training augmentation range.
Iterative application of gap filling and diagonal repair yields consistent linear score gains up to at least 5 iterations.
Gaussian filtering on a boolean mask followed by thresholding is a surprisingly effective and simple smoothing trick.
Larger training patch sizes improve Dice scores but can degrade Topo scores, requiring careful balancing.
Targeted 2x2 diagonal bridging effectively repairs specific prediction patterns with minimal risk of score drops.
critical_findings: Probability fusion and smaller thresholds (0.20-0.26) optimized for the public leaderboard actually degraded private leaderboard performance compared to logits fusion and larger thresholds (0.35-0.40).
Standard dilation plugs holes but adds too many voxels, degrading the Dice score, whereas a custom 2 x 2 x 2 neighborhood lookup table successfully plugs 1-voxel holes without over-adding.
High volume IOU does not guarantee a good topology score, as the task requires continuous surface objects rather than just volumetric overlap.
Ground truth annotations can eat into the air, indicating weak supervision that requires careful handling of unlabelled regions.
Local CV and public LB did not correlate well with the private LB, complicating submission selection.
Inferring on full 320x320x320 volumes instead of sliding windows prevents topological artifacts.
CutMix improved scores in earlier model families but provided no benefit for the final SEResNeXt152 runs.
The topological score suffers a disproportionate drop from even a small hole, making precise topological correction critical.

A metric hack involving manually zeroing a fully enclosed voxel to create a Betti-2 hole successfully improved scores, contradicting the host's claim that such bugs have limited practical impact at the top end.
PCA-based hole filling fails completely when applied to adhering sheets, requiring alternative approaches.
The original public notebook's local CV Dice (0.670) was notably lower than its LB score (0.552), but the modified pipeline closed this gap significantly (0.702 CV Dice, 0.585 official metric).
The host's suggested uuNet baseline and MedialSurfaceRecall loss are highly effective for this task.
Using a hard mask alone fails to provide useful gradients; warping a blurred mask significantly improves topology and VOI performance.
The public subset's scoring distribution was disproportionately boosted by a small number of samples, causing a discrepancy between public and private leaderboard performance.
Different diffeomorphic implementations optimized for different metrics (Surface Dice vs. Topology/VOI), highlighting the need for metric-specific validation.
Label 2 voxels at the 3-voxel volume faces consistently cause artifacts if not explicitly zeroed out before postprocessing.
Global interpolation pipelines risk dipping into label 2 territory, making local patch-based methods superior for this specific data layout.
High logits thresholds separate sheets but degrade overall segmentation quality, while low thresholds merge them; combining multiple thresholds resolves this trade-off.
Pseudo-labeling with external unlabeled data provided no significant performance improvement and was ultimately abandoned.
Increasing training rotation augmentations beyond ±30° actually degraded performance, making ±15° TTA the optimal choice.
Increasing patch size to 224 or 256 improved Dice but caused Topo to drop, making 192 the optimal balance.
The line normalization step must ignore pixels within 8 voxels of block borders to avoid artifacts near ignore masks.
Complex adhesion-separation methods relying on high-confidence seeds fail when the model already merges distinct objects into single components.
what_did_not_work: The team could not find an effective solution to the problem of touching sheets and had to rely on the model to minimize their occurrence.
SwinUNETR, UNETR++, MedNeXt, SegFormer3D, ConvNeXt V2, U-Mamba, and SEResNeXt200 did not surpass the SEResNeXt152 + AttUNet architecture.
Topology-aware loss variants like persistence diagram matching and Euler characteristic loss were too slow to train effectively.
PCA-based hole filling fails on adhering sheets.
End-to-end RBF function training and point cloud separation models were considered but not implemented.
Skeaw-BorT-Loss failed because the background cannot be split into components.
Medialsurface loss improved topo scores but decreased surface dice.
A global interpolation pipeline scored well on private LB but failed on public LB/CV due to label 2 artifacts.
Pseudo-labeling with external/unlabeled data did not bring significant performance improvements.
Larger rotation augmentations during training led to performance degradation.
Enforced minimum sheet thickness destroyed topology and failed on small samples.
Binary closing and closing+hole patching+cavity fill yielded lower scores than median filtering.
2D slice line interpolation/endpoint pairing was abandoned during the competition because it made predictions worse on certain cases.

The marker-controlled watershed adhesion separation method suffered from seed dependency when high-confidence predictions were already merged, and its fixed 2-pixel gap removal introduced more holes than it resolved.
notable_individual_insights: rank 1 (1st Place Solution for the Vesuvius Challenge - Surface Detection Competition): Optimizing for the public leaderboard can be misleading; larger thresholds and logits fusion proved superior for the private leaderboard.
rank 2 (2nd Place Solution Vesuvius Challenge – A postprocessing win): The topology metric relies on persistence homology, meaning component location matters as much as component count.
rank 3 (Bronze Medal - ChatGPT Vibe Coding!): ChatGPT can effectively generate custom PyTorch architectures and loss functions when provided with host specifications and community comments.
rank 4 (4-th Place Solution): Manipulating Betti-2 counts can effectively balance topological metrics despite official claims of limited impact.
rank 5 (5th Place Solution): SDF regression outperforms binary classification for optimizing topology and boundary metrics.
rank 9 (9th Place Solution SUPER-CONSERVATIVE(0.623->0.616)): Iterative application of gap filling and diagonal repair yields consistent linear score gains up to at least 5 iterations.
rank 10 (10th place solution): Intentionally introducing errors via low thresholds and Gaussian blur helps the diffeomorphic model learn to correct topology and thickness.
